speakers.name: Varun Berry
direct_answers: Yes, yes, so he was talking about EBITDA, I was talking about PAT.
Sorry, what -- I guess, our growths are coming through the strength of the brands as well as our distribution efforts. And we hope to continue with that.
We've always been very conscious of our overhead costs, but no, there's no reorganization.

So we focus on making sure that our top line growths, which I must confess that we've not been able to do in the last 2 years, but we -- our principle is that our -- if our top line growth is X, our fixed cost growth or our people cost growth should be 0.75X. And that's what we will keep with, so that we are leveraging our top line growths.
No, there is no flexibility. It's just the planning. You've got to plan for -- yes.
No, cost savings are going very well for us. In fact, we probably will beat our targets that we've set for ourselves for the full year.

It's about 5.5%. So not a big difference. There's a delta, because as I said, the adjacency business is much smaller for us but growing faster. So the delta between the total volume growth and the biscuit volume growth is 0.8%, 0.9%.

Yes, for sure. The exit numbers are certainly better. And even if you were to look at some of the public declarations on growths, etcetera, by some of the companies, it seems that we are ahead. Obviously, Nielsen gives us certain numbers, but they are based on taking a sample. When you look at the numbers declared by some of the public companies, it seems that we are certainly doing better than the others.

No, so we were measured because we were not clear where the inflationary trends are going from a deflation to inflation. And we were also hopeful -- actually not just us but the entire industry was hopeful that maybe the duties on fat, etcetera, will go away. But as our Honorable Finance Minister has said, this is basically going to stay because they want to indigenize fats in India.

Well, not so much. We've been -- yes, we did some forward buying, which helped us, as I said, to about 2% or 3%. But now with the season coming on Wheat, March, the season starts. So hopefully, we will see some better prices as far as flour is concerned, and some normalization. So I think it should be okay.
dodged_or_vague: We will stay in the current range, I would say. That would be the range that we would look at, although we don't give any forward-looking forecast, but I would say current range is what we would target.

We don't give any forward estimates. But as I had said, we are taking 6% to 6.5% price increase, 2.5% is going to be the cost efficiencies that we are going to work on. And there will be other opportunities that we are looking at, at this point in time. So we will make sure. We've been doing this forever, and we will also make sure this time that we will be able to provide the right growth as well as the right profitability to the business.
speakers.summary: Varun Berry, the Vice Chairman and Managing Director of Britannia Industries, presented a confident outlook during the earnings call. He highlighted the company's 6.5% revenue growth amidst challenging inflationary conditions, with a focus on strategic pillars such as distribution expansion and cost leadership. Berry emphasized the company's proactive approach to managing inflation through strategic price increases and cost efficiencies. He addressed questions directly, particularly on financial metrics and strategic initiatives, while maintaining a cautious stance on forward-looking forecasts. Overall, Berry conveyed a strong commitment to sustaining market share and profitability despite external challenges.

speakers.name: Ayush Agarwal
speakers.summary: Ayush Agarwal, representing the Investor Relations team, opened the Britannia Industries earnings call with a neutral tone. He introduced the key management team members present on the call and provided logistical details about the analyst deck and the Safe Harbor Statement. Agarwal's role was primarily to facilitate the transition to the main presentation by Varun Berry, ensuring that participants were aware of the resources available for the call. His contribution was focused on setting the stage for the detailed financial discussion that followed.
